segments.text:
Levels of Complexity in Living Systems
1  atoms
2  molecules
3  cells
4  tissues
5  organs
6  organisms
7  populations
8  communities and ecosystems
9  biosphere
L102 is mostly #1 - #6
L101 is mostly #6 - #9

An animal is:
cells   tissue
tissues     organ
organs    organ system

surface area = 6
volume = 1
surface : volume ratio is 6:1
surface area = 24
volume = 8
surface : volume ratio is 24:8 or 3:1

A choanocyte or choanoflagellate
flagellum
“collar” of microvilli

Codosiga
Proterospongia

Colonial choanoflagellate
sponge – Phylum Porifera

specialization
interdependence
closest relatives

Characters used in classification:
morphology
developmental patterns
molecular characters – DNA sequence
A phylogeny based on morphology – a really bad idea!

A phylogeny based on molecular data
much more likely

Your book’s version
(Figure 30.2)
accurate,
but upside-down
What we’ll use

Evolution is NOT progressive
deeper branches = more ancient lineages
more recent branches sometimes = more complex
more recent ≠ “better”, “higher”, “more evolved”

Major points of divergence
(morphological and developmental characters):
symmetry
germ layers
embryonic formation of gut
larval form
growth control

Types of symmetry - asymmetry
no symmetry
no defined body plan

Types of symmetry – radial symmetry
1 axis = oral – aboral axis

Types of symmetry – bilateral symmetry
3 axes:   anterior – posterior
dorsal – ventral
left – right
cephalic structure

Germ Layers
2 germ layers = diploblastic
ectoderm = outer layer
endoderm = inner layer
3 germ layers = triploblastic
mesoderm = middle layer
